Some setup before we get to the code.
and click "Go to console" in the top right.
A full-screen modal pops up. Enter a project name — I used "Firebase-Express". Click "Continue".
Second step asks about Google Analytics. We don't need it — disable and click "Create project".
You should land on the project console.
The "Set up Cloud Storage" modal pops up. Choose "Start in production mode".
by default, no need to change. Click "Done".

If you don't plan to integrate a backend API, you can upload images directly from this UI.
But if you want backend API integration, keep going.
For uploads we need the Firebase Admin SDK.
Click "Service accounts" — you'll see the Firebase Admin SDK page.
Click "Generate new private key" (pick your language for sample code). You'll get a JSON file — save it carefully.

Don't share the key with anyone.
Don't push the key to GitHub (if you did, delete that repo and regenerate the key).
Firebase prep done. Time for code.
We'll use Express generator to create a project. Install it first if you haven't:

express --view=ejs firebase-express

cd firebase-express

npm install
: Firebase's Admin SDK — full access (auth, database, storage, etc.).
: Firebase's Client SDK — limited access (auth, storage, etc.).

FIREBASE_TYPE=
FIREBASE_PROJECT_ID=
FIREBASE_PRIVATE_KEY_ID=
FIREBASE_PRIVATE_KEY=
FIREBASE_CLIENT_EMAIL=
FIREBASE_CLIENT_ID=
FIREBASE_AUTH_URI=
FIREBASE_TOKEN_URI=
FIREBASE_AUTH_PROVIDER_X509_CERT_URL=
FIREBASE_CLIENT_X509_CERT_URL=
These map to the JSON from "Get the Firebase Admin SDK". Fill them in.

Upload via Postman and check Firebase Storage — your file should appear.
We uploaded the file but didn't return its URL. Modify the code to return the URL on success:
Signed URLs require permission and expiration. I deliberately set 2500 — essentially permanent.
Error: The expiration date provided was invalid.
Test again with Postman — you'll see the URL in the response.

Firebase Storage is actually built on Google Cloud Storage. That's why the Firebase Admin docs only cover getting started and not uploads — Google Cloud Storage handles that part. The Firebase docs note:

You can use the bucket references returned by the Admin SDK in conjunction with the official Google Cloud Storage client libraries to upload, download, and modify content in the buckets associated with your Firebase projects. Note that you do not have to authenticate Google Cloud Storage libraries when using the Firebase Admin SDK. The bucket references returned by the Admin SDK are already authenticated with the credentials used to initialize your Firebase app.

So far we've learned upload, get URL, delete, and list. These are the basics. But we still need to handle filename collisions and compression. Let's continue.
Renaming is straightforward. Common naming conventions:
Let's use UUID — Universally Unique Identifier. Collision probability is near zero.
Files now get renamed on upload.
Now compression. Plenty of options exist, but I'll use TinyPNG — it can compress to 1/3 of original size with minimal quality loss.
The free TinyPNG plan allows 500 images per month.
You'll get an email with the TinyPNG link.
Click in to the TinyPNG dashboard. Copy the API Key.
Add the API Key to env:
// Upload compressed image to Firebase Storage
Check Firebase Storage — your images are now compressed. Below: 21.47kb uncompressed vs 6.78kb compressed.
TinyPNG can also resize images — see
